3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Singh, G., Sajjad, O. M., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Jackson, D. B., Assari, S., Murray, S. B., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Baker, F. C. (2022). Social epidemiology of early adolescent problematic screen use in the United States. Pediatric Research, 92, 1443-1449.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We conducted a secondary cross-sectional analysis of data from the 2-year follow-up of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (4.0 release)... After omitting study participants who were not asked problematic screen use questions due to not reporting video game, social media, or mobile phone use (n = 3122), 8753 children remained in the analytic sample.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Different subsets for each outcome: video games n=7595, social media n=5652, mobile phone n=7361

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Sociodemographic factors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parent education, parent marital status)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - exposure is sociodemographic, outcome is problematic screen use
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Parents reported participants' sex (male or female), country of birth (US or outside US), primary language (English or non-English), and race/ethnicity (non-Latinx/Hispanic white, non-Latinx/Hispanic Black, Native American, Latinx/Hispanic, Asian, or Other) at baseline. In addition, parents reported highest parent education and household income at Year 2.
3C_Exposure.notes: This paper uses sociodemographics as predictors and problematic screen use as outcome - opposite direction from most papers in this review. Screen time is both an independent variable (total recreational screen time) and context for the outcome variables.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Problematic video game use (VGAQ), problematic social media use (SMAQ), and problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ): The six-question VGAQ was used to assess problematic video game use as reported by adolescents... Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ): used to assess problematic social media use... Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ): The eight-question MPIQ was designed to assess problematic mobile phone use as reported by adolescents.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple linear regression with propensity weights; interaction terms for income x race/ethnicity
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parent education, parent marital status, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate cross-sectional associations between sociodemographic factors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language of the child, household income, parents' highest education, parent marital status) and three forms of problematic screen use (video game, social media, mobile phone), adjusting for the site.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Male B=0.74 (0.69 to 0.79), p<0.001 for video games
Table 3: Male B=-0.11 (-0.16 to -0.05), p<0.001 for social media
Table 3: Male B=-0.18 (-0.24 to -0.12), p<0.001 for mobile phones
Table 3: Black B=0.17 (0.10 to 0.25), p<0.001 for video games
Table 3: Less than $75,000 B=0.15 (0.07 to 0.23), p<0.001 for mobile phones

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: It is also important to note that the effect sizes of some sociodemographic factors were relatively small.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Digital literacy education strategies can focus on at-risk populations, encourage targeted counseling by pediatricians, and adapt family media use plans for diverse backgrounds.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on documenting sociodemographic disparities in problematic screen use, noting sex differences (boys higher on video games, girls higher on social media/phone), racial/ethnic differences, and income effects. It links these to diminished returns theory for minority groups. The paper appropriately notes small effect sizes and cross-sectional design limitations. Recommendations focus on tailoring digital literacy education for at-risk groups rather than universal screen time reduction.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: The exposure-outcome relationship is reversed from most papers in this review (sociodemographics predict problematic screen use, rather than screen time predicting health outcomes). The primary screen time-to-outcome pathway is less direct.
